data.title: GSK wins EU approval for drug combos containing blood cancer drug Blenrep
data.description:
GSK (NYSE:GSK) announced on Thursday that the EU regulators approved two combination therapies involving its antibody drug conjugate, Blenrep, developed with Pfizer (PFE) and Kyowa Kirin (OTCPK:KYKOF) (OTCPK:KYKOY), for multiple myeloma.

Specifically, the approval allows the British drugmaker to market Blenrep with bortezomib, an anticancer drug marketed as Velcade by Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), and with pomalidomide, marketed by Bristol Myers (BMY) as Pomalyst.

Each combination, indicated for patients with relapsed or refractory MM as a late-line option, should include dexamethasone, a widely used anti-inflammatory agent.

The approval comes a day after the FDA extended the review period for a Biologics License Application submitted by GSK (NYSE:GSK) seeking U.S. approval for similar Blenrep combinations targeted at R/R MM.

Last week, a group of independent advisors to the FDA voted against the treatments, citing concerns over their benefit-risk profiles.

data.title: CTS Corporation maintains $520M-$550M sales guidance as diversification fuels growth amid tariff and market headwinds
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: CTS Corporation (CTS) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Kieran M. O'Sullivan emphasized, "We delivered another quarter of double-digit growth in our diversified end markets. Diversified sales for the quarter were 55% of overall company revenue." He highlighted that adjusted EBITDA expanded 250 basis points sequentially and 130 basis points versus the prior year, with strong cash flow generation and continued execution on the diversification strategy targeting medical, industrial, aerospace, and defense sectors.
  • O'Sullivan announced the launch of the next-generation smart actuator for the commercial vehicle market and pointed to the SyQwest acquisition as a key driver for the 13% year-over-year growth in diversified end market sales. He noted transportation sales declined 6% due to China market headwinds.
  • CFO Ashish Agrawal stated, "Sales in the second quarter were $135 million, up 8% sequentially and up 4% from the last year. Sales to diversified end markets increased 13% year-over-year. SyQwest added $4.5 million in revenue during the quarter."
  • Agrawal also reported, "Our adjusted gross margin was 38.7% in the second quarter, up 296 basis points compared to the second quarter of 2024 and up 174 basis points sequentially compared to the first quarter of 2025."

Outlook

  • O'Sullivan reiterated, "Assuming the continuation of current market conditions, we are maintaining our guidance of sales in the range of $520 million to $550 million and adjusted diluted EPS to be in the range of $2.20 to $2.35."
  • The company expects medical end market demand to be mixed, with strength in therapeutics and softness in diagnostics. Aerospace and defense revenue is anticipated to grow, driven by SyQwest momentum. Industrial sales are projected to improve, while transportation revenue is expected to face softness due to trade tariffs and China market dynamics.
  • O'Sullivan cautioned, "We are monitoring the potential for headwinds in transportation revenue due to trade tariffs and the China market dynamics. We anticipate softness in commercial vehicle-related revenue for the remainder of the year."

Financial Results

  • CTS reported Q2 sales of $135 million, up from $130 million in the same quarter last year. Adjusted diluted earnings were $0.57 per share, up from $0.44 in Q1 2025 and $0.54 in Q2 2024. Reported earnings per diluted share for the quarter were $0.62.
  • Operating cash flow for Q2 was $28 million, compared to $20 million in the prior year quarter, and the year-to-date figure was $44 million. The company closed Q2 with a cash balance of $99 million and long-term debt of $88 million.
  • CTS returned $26 million to shareholders so far in 2025 through dividends and share buybacks, including the repurchase of 412,000 shares for approximately $17 million in Q2. $38 million remains under the current share repurchase program.
  • Adjusted EBITDA margin reached 23%, up 250 basis points sequentially and 130 basis points year-over-year.

Q&A

  • John Edward Franzreb, Sidoti: Asked about the medical market, noting divergence between therapeutic and diagnostic product lines. O'Sullivan responded, "On the therapeutics side, on the last earnings call, we talked about a larger order, and that's obviously playing out here in the second quarter and for the balance of the year as well. We've seen some softness in diagnostics on the ultrasound side... mostly coming from capital spend in Asia and maybe tariff related as well."
  • Franzreb: Queried the impact of tariffs. Agrawal replied, "Overall, the impact is very, very minimal in the quarter."
  • Franzreb: Probed outlook for transportation market bottoming. O'Sullivan said, "We think it's bottomed out in China, but we're being a little bit just cautious on that... It feels a little tenuous for the next few quarters."
  • Franzreb: Asked about SyQwest integration and cost actions. O'Sullivan noted, "A lot of the integration work has been done. There's still a little bit more to do. We're still running on different ERP systems." Agrawal added, "We are really not looking to drive cost saving type of actions [at SyQwest]... we are shoring up capabilities from a production standpoint."
  • Hendi Susanto, Gabelli Funds: Asked about sustainability of large medical therapeutic orders and diagnostic softness. O'Sullivan answered, "The softness in diagnostics maybe for a quarter or 2, we still feel very confident in the growth of that business in the mid- to long term."
  • Susanto: Inquired about SyQwest prior-year revenue. Agrawal responded, "We closed on the acquisition towards the end of July last year. So the second quarter of 2025 was purely additive."
  • Susanto: Asked about transportation pipeline and regional focus. O'Sullivan said, "We would be working with customers on accelerator modules, passive safety sensors, new combination sensors, motor position sensing... the larger pipeline is in the North America region for sure."
  • Franzreb: Asked about acquisition market activity. O'Sullivan: "We're most focused on advancing our diversified end markets... we certainly see some uptick in activity out there."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts displayed a neutral to slightly cautious tone, focusing on the divergence within the medical market, the impact of tariffs, timing of transportation recovery, and integration progress for SyQwest. There was interest in acquisition opportunities but no urgent skepticism.
  • Management maintained a confident and steady tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, emphasizing resilience: "We remain confident in the longer-term growth prospects for this product line," and, "We feel very confident in the growth of that business in the mid- to long term."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's confidence in diversification and margin expansion remained strong, with slightly more caution around transportation and tariff impacts. Analysts' tone was consistent, focusing on operational questions and forward projections.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Sales increased from $126 million in Q1 to $135 million in Q2 2025, with adjusted diluted earnings per share rising from $0.44 to $0.57.
  • Book-to-bill ratio for diversified end markets was 1, down from 1.28 in Q1, indicating moderation in new orders. Sequential gross margin improved from 37% to 38.7%.
  • Transportation sales decline moderated, and Q2 saw the launch of a next-generation smart actuator for commercial vehicles. SyQwest revenue contribution increased from $3 million to $4.5 million.
  • Management maintained guidance and focus on diversification, while reiterating caution on tariffs and regional macro headwinds. Analysts continued to press on medical and transportation dynamics, with ongoing interest in cost controls and acquisition strategy.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified ongoing risks from trade tariffs, geopolitical volatility, and the China market. O'Sullivan said, "The impact of tariffs and the geopolitical environment are creating uncertainty."
  • Transportation revenue is flagged for potential softness for the remainder of 2025 due to tariffs and Chinese market issues.
  • Diagnostic ultrasound bookings in medical markets showed softness, with expectations for a short-term impact.
  • Management is closely monitoring tariffs and collaborating with customers and suppliers to adapt pricing and logistics, while SyQwest integration continues amid some remaining ERP system differences.

Final Takeaway

CTS Corporation delivered solid top-line growth and margin expansion in Q2 2025, supported by its diversification strategy in medical, industrial, aerospace, and defense markets. The company maintained its annual guidance of $520 million to $550 million in sales and adjusted diluted EPS of $2.20 to $2.35, while highlighting continued vigilance on tariff and regional market uncertainties. Management's outlook remains confident in long-term growth prospects, especially in diversified markets, with a strong balance sheet positioned to support both organic and acquisition-driven expansion.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: American Airlines outlines $0.20–$0.80 FY25 EPS range as domestic demand softens, targets full indirect channel share recovery by end of 2025
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: American Airlines (AAL) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Robert D. Isom reported "an adjusted pretax profit of $869 million for the second quarter or earnings per share of $0.95, which is toward the high end of the guidance we provided in April." Isom highlighted "record revenue of $14.4 billion" and noted that "our year-over-year passenger unit revenue improvement led our network peers for the fourth straight quarter." He stated that premium demand "remained resilient," with the premium cabin unit revenue performing "4 points better than the main cabin." Isom acknowledged weakness in domestic leisure, with domestic unit revenue "down approximately 6% year-over-year as softness in the main cabin persisted throughout the second quarter." He said, "we expect that July will be the low point and that performance will improve sequentially each month in the quarter as industry capacity growth slows and demand strengthens."

  • Isom discussed indirect channel recovery, stating "our indirect share now down 3% versus historical levels" and reaffirmed, "we remain on track to get back to our historical share of indirect channel revenue as we exit 2025."

  • Isom provided updates on the AAdvantage loyalty program, noting "active AAdvantage members have grown 7% year-to-date" and spending on co-branded credit cards was "up 6% year-over-year."

  • On the operational front, Isom said, "there was significant storm activity at our hubs... a 36% increase in disruptive operational events over the same period last year," but credited "investments we've made in technology and our operations" with enabling rapid recovery.

  • CFO Devon E. May stated, "excluding net special items, American reported a second quarter operating margin of approximately 8% and earnings per share of $0.95, both at the high end of our guidance." May credited "the team's continued strong execution on our efficiency initiatives and a shift in timing of maintenance events to later in the year." May added, "we expect to have driven cumulative savings of over $750 million and delivered approximately $600 million of working capital improvements since we launched our reengineering the business efforts in 2023."

Outlook

  • May provided third quarter expectations: "we expect capacity to be up 2% to 3% year-over-year" and "third quarter revenue to be between down 2% and up 1% year-over-year." He projected, "we expect to produce a third quarter loss per share of between $0.10 and $0.60."

  • For the full year, May said, "we expect full year earnings per share of between a loss of $0.20 and a profit of $0.80, with the midpoint being a profit of $0.30 per share."

  • May noted, "we believe the top end of the range is achievable if demand in the domestic market continues to strengthen, and we would only expect to be at the bottom end of the range if there was macro weakness that we don't see in our recent booking trends."

Financial Results

  • Reported "second quarter revenue of $14.4 billion was up 0.4% year-over-year."

  • "Second quarter unit cost, excluding fuel and net special items, was up 3.4% year-over-year, over 0.5 point better than the midpoint of our guidance," according to May.

  • American "ended the second quarter with approximately $38 billion of total debt and $29 billion of net debt, our lowest net debt levels since the third quarter of 2015." The company "ended the second quarter with $12 billion of total available liquidity."

  • May said, "we produced $791 million of free cash flow and have now produced $2.5 billion of free cash flow in the first half of the year."

  • For the fleet, May stated, "we now expect to take delivery of 50 new aircraft this year at the high end of our previous range of 40 to 50 deliveries." 2025 aircraft CapEx is "now expected to be between $2.5 billion and $3 billion, and our total CapEx is expected to be between $3.5 billion and $4 billion."

Q&A

  • Jamie Nathaniel Baker, JPMorgan: Asked about the percentage of flights operating at a loss and paths to reducing loss-producing flying. Isom replied, "We don't run our airline based on other airlines' perceptions... the primary differentiator between us and some of our competitors is largely... we're paying our team members at market wages... We do have a network... more oriented to the domestic network. The domestic network has been under stress... We think that's going to change."

  • Conor T. Cunningham, Melius Research: Queried about sequential improvement in U.S. domestic market and future earnings potential. Isom said, "July has been tough... as we take a look through the third quarter, we probably have about 65% of revenue on the books... a lot of optimism for some of the trends that we're seeing going from July into August and September and into the fourth quarter." May added, "July is going to look a lot like our second quarter results. But as we get into August and September, we're going to see some nice improving trends."

  • Catherine Maureen O'Brien, Goldman Sachs: Asked about capacity and unit costs versus January, and indirect revenue share. May responded, "This year is largely unfolding as we expected to start the year... At the midpoint, unit costs up probably somewhere around 3.5%." On indirect share, Isom confirmed "as we exit 2025, we're on track to getting -- restoring our full indirect channel share."

  • Atul Maheswari, UBS: Asked about margin gaps to peers and closing the gap. May noted, "There's a gap today, and there will be a gap this year, but we do expect to close it heading into 2026."

  • Other analysts raised questions on Chicago expansion, Embraer aircraft tariffs, network growth, corporate demand, customer experience initiatives, and the impact of weather disruptions. Management consistently emphasized ongoing efficiency, recovery in indirect channel share, and investments in premium and international growth.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed on domestic demand weakness and margin gaps, showing a neutral to slightly negative tone, particularly around domestic exposure and relative underperformance versus peers.

  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks, emphasizing resilience, efficiency, and recovery trends. During Q&A, tone shifted to a more defensive stance when addressing competitive challenges and margin gaps but remained optimistic about sequential improvement and indirect channel recovery.

  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's confidence on indirect channel share recovery was higher, and analysts continued to focus on domestic weakness and competitive gaps.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance in Q2 is more specific, providing a range for full year EPS, compared to guidance withdrawal in Q1.

  • Q2 call features stronger emphasis on indirect channel recovery and premium product expansion, with management stating plans to "restore our full indirect channel share" by year-end 2025.

  • Domestic revenue weakness remained a concern in both quarters, but management in Q2 projects sequential improvement and highlights international and premium strength.

  • Analysts in both quarters focused on capacity, margin gaps, and domestic demand, though in Q2, there was increased scrutiny on the path to closing the margin gap with peers.

  • Management tone in Q2 was more optimistic about operational recovery and efficiency, while still acknowledging competitive and demand headwinds.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management pointed to "significant storm activity" at several hubs, leading to operational disruptions and a "36% increase in disruptive operational events over the same period last year."

  • Domestic leisure demand softness continues to pressure unit revenue, with Isom noting "domestic unit revenue was down approximately 6% year-over-year."

  • May cited "the full financial cost of new collective bargaining agreements and a material drop in demand in the domestic market where we produce over 70% of our revenue" as major challenges.

  • Analysts flagged ongoing margin gaps to network peers, concerns about capacity growth, and exposure to domestic market volatility.

Final Takeaway

American Airlines management emphasized progress on efficiency and indirect channel recovery, with a renewed focus on premium products and operational resilience. While domestic softness remains a headwind, the company forecasts sequential improvement through the second half of the year and aims to restore full indirect channel share by year-end 2025. Management projects full year EPS between a loss of $0.20 and a profit of $0.80, pointing to premium and international segments as key drivers for future margin expansion and revenue growth.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: WEX outlines 0.5% to 1% revenue boost in first year post-BP portfolio conversion while prioritizing debt reduction
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: WEX Inc. (WEX) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Melissa D. Smith, Chair and CEO, reported that "we delivered stronger financial results in the second quarter than anticipated with revenue at the top end of our guidance and adjusted EPS exceeding guidance." The company announced several customer wins, including BP in Mobility, The United Auto Workers Trust in Benefits, and a large new corporate payments customer. Smith stated, "Our continued ability to win top-tier customers underscores the strength of our offerings." The BP partnership is expected to add 0.5% to 1% to company revenue in the first full year after conversion, with new sales to begin in Q4 and portfolio conversion anticipated for 2026.
  • Smith highlighted that in Mobility, transaction levels were down slightly, and same-store sales growth for local fleets in the U.S. declined, while over-the-road customers saw a modest decline of less than 1%. She emphasized strong new sales momentum, especially among small businesses, and noted, "The investments we're making in digital marketing targeted at small businesses are bearing the fruit we expected."
  • In Benefits, SaaS account growth was 6%, and HSA accounts grew by 7% in Q2, now totaling more than 8.7 million. Smith added, "WEX Technologies powers over 20% of the total HSA market through both our direct and partner offerings."
  • Corporate Payments segment revenues were impacted by a large travel customer transition, but Smith noted, "The platform investments we're making to diversify from travel have started to bear fruit, and we're seeing strength in our new customer pipeline."
  • Jagtar Narula, CFO, stated, "Total revenue in the quarter was $659.6 million, which is down 2% versus last year." He added, "Adjusted earnings per share of $3.95 was an increase of 1% year-over-year." Narula confirmed that cost controls and higher fuel prices contributed positively and that guidance now reflects only new sales from the BP contract for 2025, with conversion gains not yet included.

Outlook

  • Full year 2025 revenue is expected to be in the range of $2.61 billion to $2.65 billion, with adjusted net income EPS between $15.37 and $15.77 per diluted share. For Q3, revenue guidance is $669 million to $689 million, and adjusted net income EPS is $4.30 to $4.50 per diluted share. Narula clarified, "We have only included revenue related to new sales from the BP contract in guidance and none related to the accounts once converted."
  • The company expects Corporate Payments revenue to return to growth in the second half of 2025, starting in Q3 and accelerating in Q4. Mobility is projected to maintain trends similar to the first half, with investments in sales and marketing expected to drive future reacceleration.

Financial Results

  • WEX reported Q2 revenue of $659.6 million, a 2.1% year-over-year decline, flat when excluding fuel price and FX impacts. Adjusted EPS was $3.95, up 1% year-over-year, and 8% growth excluding fuel and FX fluctuations.
  • Mobility segment revenue declined 3.7%, with a 4.2% drag from fuel prices and FX. Payment processing rate increased to 1.31%.
  • Benefits segment revenue grew 8.5% year-over-year to $195.1 million, with custodial investment revenue at $57.8 million, up 11.4%.
  • Corporate Payments revenue was $118.3 million, down 11.8% year-over-year due to the travel customer transition. Direct AP volume grew more than 25% versus last year.
  • The leverage ratio ended the quarter at 3.4x. Narula said, "For the remainder of this year, we will prioritize using available cash flow to pay down debt and reduce leverage."

Q&A

  • Shray Gurtata, Barclays: Asked about new product initiatives in Direct AP and reacceleration timeline in Mobility. Smith responded that investments are increasing mobile capability and "there's a real hunger...for someone who can do that end-to-end processing," while reacceleration in Mobility will take time as cost discipline and fuel efficiency trends persist.
  • Nikolai Chrin Cremo, UBS: Inquired about the impact of same-store sales trends and BP portfolio conversion timing. Narula confirmed, "we're expecting the second half of the year from a growth perspective to look a lot like the first half of the year in the Mobility segment." Smith indicated BP portfolio conversion is expected in 2026, with costs already embedded in guidance.
  • Christopher Nathaniel Svensson, Deutsche Bank: Sought clarity on Corporate Payments KPIs and sustainability of HSA growth. Smith cited normalization post-travel client transition and noted "deep expertise...the scale of our platform" are driving HSA outperformance.
  • Sanjay Sakhrani, KBW: Asked about long-term revenue growth as company laps OTA transition. Narula said, "we're kind of going from, call it, flattish growth in the first half of the year to 4% growth second half of the year."
  • Rayna Kumar, Oppenheimer: Queried on travel trends and operating margin outlook. Smith described travel volume as stable and growing in non-U.S. corridors. Narula said, "adjusted operating margin...we kind of expect to be in line with what we saw in the second quarter."
  • David John Koning, Baird: Pressed on Mobility processing rates and Benefits processing/account servicing growth. Narula expects processing rates to tick up and highlighted the impact from interest rates in H2.
  • Trevor Ellis Williams, Jefferies: Asked about strategic review appetite and Corporate Payments wallet share. Smith said the Board "regularly reviews the composition of our business portfolio...to deliver the best returns for our shareholders."
  • Andrew William Jeffrey, William Blair: Probed on EV adoption in Mobility and fintech virtual card competition. Smith reported a slower EV ramp but strong product positioning, and confidence in virtual card technology's market appeal.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were neutral to slightly positive, focusing on growth pipelines, the BP partnership, and pace of recovery in Mobility and Corporate Payments. Skepticism centered on the sustainability of recent wins and timing of reacceleration.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone, especially in prepared remarks, emphasizing, "Our robust new customer pipeline gives me confidence that the investments we're making in sales and marketing are paying off" (Smith). In Q&A, management was cautious, clarifying assumptions and timelines, particularly around Mobility and BP conversion.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone was more upbeat regarding sales pipeline and product investments, while analysts retained a similar level of scrutiny but acknowledged recent customer wins.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for revenue and adjusted EPS increased slightly from Q1, with Q2 results benefitting from higher fuel prices and cost controls.
  • Strategic focus shifted from macroeconomic uncertainty in Q1 to customer acquisition and product investment in Q2, notably the BP win and a strengthened Corporate Payments pipeline.
  • Analysts in both quarters focused on Mobility trends, Corporate Payments recovery, and Benefits account growth, but Q2 questions were more detailed on timing and magnitude of future growth.
  • Management's confidence increased, citing strong pipelines and early returns on sales and marketing investments.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing macro uncertainty, persistent softness in Mobility same-store sales, and transition-related headwinds in Corporate Payments.
  • Tariff-driven volatility and fuel price/FX fluctuations remain risks, with mitigation through cost controls and diversified product investments.
  • Analysts questioned timing of revenue acceleration, impact of BP portfolio conversion, and the sustainability of margins given ongoing investments.

Final Takeaway

WEX management emphasized that strategic investments in product innovation and sales and marketing are yielding tangible returns, highlighted by marquee wins like BP and robust pipelines in all segments. The company reiterated its focus on disciplined cost management, debt reduction, and leveraging its unique platform strengths to drive durable revenue growth and margin expansion into 2026 and beyond.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: LVMH Moët Hennessy reports 22% y-o-y decline in net profit to €5.7B
data.description:
• LVMH Moët Hennessy press release (OTCPK:LVMHF): 1H revenue of €39.8 billion.
  • Profit from recurring operations for the first half of 2025 came to €9 billion, equating to an operating margin of 22.6%.
  • The Group share of net profit amounted to €5.7 billion down from €7.27B in 1H24.
  • An interim dividend of €5.50 per share will be paid on Thursday, December 4, 2025.

data.title: Long-term mortgage rates essentially flat W/W
data.description:
This week, long-term mortgage rates essentially remained flat week-over-week, the latest Freddie Mac Primary Mortgage Survey showed.

30-year fixed-rate mortgages averaged 6.74% as of July 24, down from 6.75% last week and 6.78% in the same period a year ago.

15-year fixed-rate mortgages averaged 5.87%, compared to 5.92% a week ago and 6.07% in the year-ago week.

"Overall, the backdrop for the housing market is positive as the economy continues to perform well with solid employment and income growth," said Freddie Mac's chief economist, Sam Khater.

Below is a snapshot of the week's survey results:

data.title: KeyCorp cut to Neutral at Citi on full valuation after post-earnings bump
data.description:
Citi downgraded KeyCorp (NYSE:KEY) to Neutral from Buy on Thursday after the bank's stock outperformed following its guidance boost for 2025 net interest income and net interest margin.

"We see a path to 2.75-2.8% exit-rate NIM, implying upside to NII guide, and expect management to hit fee, expense, and credit targets in ’25, but we believe this outlook is now largely priced in," analyst Keith Horowitz wrote in a note to clients.

KeyCorp's (NYSE:KEY) stock is trading at an implied ~10.5% cost of equity, in line with Citi's normalized assumption, and Horowitz's estimate for 2025 exit-rate NII in line with the consensus, he said.

Citi's Neutral rating on KEY contrasts with the SA Quant rating, average SA analyst rating, and the average Wall Street rating, all at Buy.

data.title: Selective Insurance raises 2025 combined ratio guidance to 97%-98% amid broad-based social inflationary pressures
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Selective Insurance Group, Inc. (SIGI) Q2 2025

Management View

  • John Joseph Marchioni, CEO, stated the company delivered an operating return on equity of 10.3% for the quarter, highlighting a strong 18% increase in investment income from the prior year period. He reported "Excess and surplus and Personal lines produced strong results in the quarter, with both segments reporting quarterly and year-to-date combined ratios at or below our 95% long-term target."
  • Marchioni also noted overall insurance segment growth of 5%, attributing it to "our disciplined underwriting and pricing strategy in an increasingly competitive market." He addressed the impact of unfavorable prior year casualty reserve development, which amounted to $45 million, pushing the overall combined ratio to 100.2%.
  • The CEO emphasized the company's proactive approach to reserve reviews, benchmarking, and loss trend monitoring, stating the reserving actions stem from industry-wide pressures, particularly in casualty lines due to social inflation. He explained, "We have been steadily increasing our loss trend estimates for casualty lines in recent years, largely in anticipation of social inflationary impacts on claim severities."
  • Marchioni described various underwriting and claims initiatives, including tightening guidelines, managing limits, reducing underperforming classes, and focusing on diversification within Commercial Lines. He said, "We continue to invest in diversifying our business mix and geographic footprint."
  • Patrick Sean Brennan, CFO, reported, "For the quarter, fully diluted EPS was $1.36 and non-GAAP operating EPS was $1.31. Our underwriting performance was breakeven, but our return on equity was 10.7%, and operating ROE was 10.3% due to the investment portfolio's continued strong performance."

Outlook

  • The company updated its 2025 guidance, projecting a GAAP combined ratio between 97% and 98%, which is up 1 point from prior guidance. This revision includes 6 points of catastrophe losses and the impact of prior year casualty reserve development through the second quarter, assuming no further reserve development or loss cost estimate changes.
  • After-tax net investment income guidance increased to $415 million, up from the prior $405 million. The guidance includes an effective tax rate of approximately 21.5% and weighted average shares of 61.5 million, with no further repurchases assumed.
  • Marchioni stated, "We remain focused on executing our risk management strategies while driving long-term profitable growth."

Financial Results

  • The GAAP combined ratio for the quarter was 100.2%, elevated by 3.8 points of unfavorable prior year casualty reserve development. Catastrophe losses came in at 6.7%, which was 1.7 points better than the prior year period.
  • The overall underlying combined ratio for the quarter was 89.7%, an improvement of 170 basis points from the prior year period. Year-to-date, the underlying combined ratio was 90.8%.
  • After-tax net investment income was $101 million, up 18% from a year ago, contributing 13 points of return on equity.
  • Book value per share increased 9% in the first half of the year, with debt to capital at 21.1% and $56 million remaining under the share repurchase authorization.

Q&A

  • Michael Wayne Phillips, Oppenheimer: Questioned the broad-based nature of social inflation and its impact across the company's book. Marchioni responded that "what we've been seeing and reacting to with higher paid emergence is evident across industry classifications and it's evident across geographies," emphasizing a widespread, industry-driven phenomenon rather than an issue unique to Selective.
  • Phillips followed up on general liability pricing. Marchioni clarified, "That number has actually moved on a pretty significant sequential basis ... our pricing stands at, call it, roughly 11% on GL, is negatively impacting our conversion rate on new business, and it's put a little bit of downward pressure on our retention."
  • Jian Huang, Morgan Stanley: Asked about Commercial Auto reserving assumptions. Marchioni stated, "Embedded in our expected loss ratios was an average assumed loss trend of about 8%. And ... our average renewal pricing on Commercial Auto liability was just over 10%."
  • Jon Paul Newsome, Piper Sandler: Inquired about accident year assumptions and workers' comp combined ratio. Marchioni said, "We remain comfortable because the assumed severities ... remain in line with what we're observing in the most recent accident years, which is the most elevated point of the diagonal," and noted workers' comp frequency was favorable through the first six months.
  • Michael David Zaremski, BMO: Probed whether recent reserve additions are unique to Selective. Marchioni answered, "This is not us trying to get our arms around old accident years ... what makes this different is we're talking about very recent relatively immature accident years for longer tail lines of business."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts repeatedly pressed management for clarity on reserve development, loss trend assumptions, and the broad-based nature of social inflation, showing a slightly negative to cautious tone, especially around the frequency and drivers of reserve additions and whether Selective's experience may be unique.
  • Management maintained a neutral to slightly defensive tone, especially when discussing recurring reserve charges. Marchioni's responses often emphasized confidence in the company's approach, as in, "We have high conviction in our view of the more recent accident years, and that feeds our high conviction on our pricing stance."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst skepticism increased, while management's tone shifted from confident to more defensive, particularly when addressing repeated reserve actions and industry-wide trends.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for the full-year combined ratio was raised from 96%-97% in Q1 to 97%-98% in Q2, signaling a more conservative outlook in response to ongoing reserve development.
  • Premium growth in Personal Lines contracted less sharply than in Q1, while target mass affluent business continued to grow. Commercial Lines retention fell to 83% from 85%, reflecting pricing and underwriting actions.
  • Management tone became more defensive in Q2, with increased analyst focus on the frequency and breadth of reserve additions, compared to the previous quarter's more growth-oriented discussion.
  • Analysts in Q2 were more persistent in questioning whether the reserving actions and social inflation impacts are unique to Selective or industry-wide, seeking more granularity on assumptions and outlook.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted industry-wide social inflation as a persistent challenge, particularly affecting casualty and commercial auto lines.
  • Unfavorable prior year casualty reserve development remains a significant risk, with Marchioni explaining the company's reserving processes and the immature nature of recent accident years being the key variable.
  • Analysts expressed concern about the recurrence of reserve charges and the potential for further adjustments if social inflationary trends continue.

Final Takeaway

Selective Insurance Group increased its 2025 combined ratio guidance to 97%-98% as ongoing social inflation, especially in casualty lines, continues to pressure underwriting margins. Management emphasized a proactive approach in reserving, pricing, and claims, while maintaining confidence in their ability to adapt quickly to emerging risks and drive long-term profitability. The quarter featured robust investment income and continued growth in key target segments, but was marked by caution on reserve development and competitive industry dynamics, leading to a more tempered outlook for the remainder of the year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Oppenheimer sees multiple tailwinds for Spotify, upgrades rating
data.description:
Analysts at Oppenheimer upgraded online music platform Spotify (NYSE:SPOT) to "outperform" from "perform" on Thursday.

The research firm believes the company will benefit from the secular tailwind of growing digital audio streaming adoption, and its subscription economics are "better than most believe."

They expect SPOT to add ~75M MAU per year (roughly in line with 2024/2025) from 2026 through 2030, resulting in a 2026-2030 CAGR of +8% vs. a 2024-2026 CAGR of +10%.

"Our engagement vs. cost analysis compared to other consumer subscription services implies SPOT is underpriced relative to usage," Oppenheimer said in its analysis. "Meanwhile, the SPOT individual tier price is largely in line with AAPL and AMZN, despite more exclusive content, features, and more users."

They noted that the company has meaningfully under-monetized its free tier as it tries to scale advertising. Over time, they expect SPOT to solve advertising or resort to a low fee (~$1.35/month), despite management plans to maintain a free tier.

SPOT has a price target of $800, implying an upside of 18.6%. Stock is up 1.4% by midday.
